claim_text: AD vs CN diagnosis is decodable from the full ComBat-harmonized ADNI cohort.
head: linear probe

site_scanner.detail: outcome exceeds scanner and the margin CI excludes zero (margin +0.55 [95% CI +0.52, +0.57])

brain_age.detail: brain-age R2=-3.78, MAE=9.1yr; effect retained 94% after brain-age-GAP control (Franke/Gaser standard), 85% under the stricter predicted-brain-age control

biomarker_anchor.detail: p-tau217 correlation r=+0.45 (95% CI lower +0.39, n=873)

replication.detail: held-out site perfectly separable at n_test=6 (AUC=1.00, CI width ~0) — too small to be informative; reported NA

biology_hypothesis: Survivor “AD vs CN diagnosis is decodable from the full ComBat-harmonized ADNI cohort.” routes to a amyloid-cascade (tau-driven) mechanism, keyed on p-tau217 and amyloid positivity. Plasma p-tau217: among the strongest blood AD biomarkers. AD-vs-CU AUC ~0.93-0.98; MCI-vs-CU ~0.94. Correlation with a structural probe score is MODEST (r~0.3-0.55), not redundant. SUPPORTED. The structural probe is read as a downstream imaging correlate of that molecular process, not an independent discovery.
next_experiment: Target N: ~120 complete-case subjects with the routing biomarker.
Direction: the structural probe score should track plasma p-tau217 (expected r~0.3-0.55, modest not redundant) and be enriched in amyloid-positive subjects.
Kill criterion: if the probe score shows no p-tau217 correlation (r<0.2) on the complete-case subset, the amyloid-cascade routing is wrong.
falsification: if the probe score shows no p-tau217 correlation (r<0.2) on the complete-case subset, the amyloid-cascade routing is wrong.
If the probe score's biomarker correlation disappears after residualizing against a scanner-predicting direction, the survivor was leakage after all.
If a held-out cohort fails to reproduce the effect (drop below the promotion floor), the mechanism claim is withdrawn.
caveats: Completeness: 1/5 gauntlet tests could not run; score is renormalized over the tests that did.
p-tau217 measured on a partial subset (~45% missing in a realistic cohort) — anchor is suggestive, not confirmatory.
Verdict is provisional and conditional on the scanner/site distribution of any replication cohort.
Brain-age adjustment uses a proxy control; residual aging signal cannot be excluded.
Any biomarker anchor is complete-case only and may not generalize.
Leakage is bounded, not eliminated — see the published batch-effect audits (‘Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,’ arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)).
Promotion authorizes one confirmatory experiment, not adoption of the finding.
A ‘strong candidate’ verdict is a licence to follow up, not to conclude — the referee promotes hypotheses, it does not confirm them.

The brain-age control is a proxy, not a gold standard: it is embedding-derived (this cohort's fit: R2=-3.78, MAE=9.1yr), so residual generic-aging signal can survive the adjustment and masquerade as disease-specific.
The biomarker anchor likely rests on a partial complete-case subset; with realistic p-tau217 missingness the correlation is estimated on far fewer subjects than the headline cohort.
Confirm the effective sample size per split — subject-disjoint cross-validation on a modest cohort leaves each fold thin.

The leakage test audits the embeddings with the same linear-probe family it uses for the outcome, so it bounds — but cannot fully rule out — shared confounding; this is the mechanic quantified in ‘Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models’ (arXiv:2606.09189 (2026)), which we cite rather than claim.

evidence_ledger.detail: outcome exceeds scanner and the margin CI excludes zero (margin +0.55 [95% CI +0.52, +0.57])
brain-age R2=-3.78, MAE=9.1yr; effect retained 94% after brain-age-GAP control (Franke/Gaser standard), 85% under the stricter predicted-brain-age control
p-tau217 correlation r=+0.45 (95% CI lower +0.39, n=873)
held-out site perfectly separable at n_test=6 (AUC=1.00, CI width ~0) — too small to be informative; reported NA
